# Representing Businesses

```{r knit-options, include=FALSE, cache=FALSE}
type = 2
load(here::here("data", type, "means.Rdata"))
here::here("code", "formatting.R") |> source()
```


> ABSTRACT: What explains unequal levels of constituency service on behalf of businesses? We find that how members represent corporate constituents is consistent with theories of committee assignments as an electoral subsidy (Grimmer and Powell 2013) and the role of committee positions in distributive politics. Legislators provide more particularistic service to businesses on behalf of agencies that their committees oversee. Also consistent with theories of distributive politics and presidential pork (Berry et al 2009), when a co-partisan comes into the White House, members appear to write more to executive-branch agencies. We plan to explore the role of corporate campaign contributions in explaining business representation.


Work on behalf of individual businesses is often on behalf of businesses in the legislators’ districts, but it may also include out-of-district campaign donors. Similar to the overall pattern, corporate service is correlated with bill sponsorship and the other components of LES scores, but this correlation is driven by the top 10% of legislators who advocate on behalf of corporate constituents.




This correlation is robust to controlling for other predictors cross-sectionally but is not statistically significant in the difference in difference model (possibly due to limited change in LES scores over time)


Also similar to the overall pattern, newly-elected legislators do less and senators do more.











Committee chairs and ranking minorities
Interestingly, ranking minority members do more corporate service, but, unlike the overall pattern, committee chairs do not.
Oversight committee membership


Our findings on how members represent corporate constituents are consistent with theories of committee assignments as an electoral subsidy and the role of committee positions in distributive politics. Legislators provide more particularistic service to businesses on behalf of agencies that their committees oversee.


However, not all of our specifications show a causal effect of joining a committee, suggesting that legislators may join committees that oversee agencies that they already contact frequently on behalf of corporate constituents.


Majority status
Members of the majority did more work on behalf of corporate constituents on average. However, this does not appear to be a causal effect. More likely it is the result of which party was in the majority and their different levels of service in our timeframe.


Co-partisan presidents


In contrast, there are no cross-sectional differences between members in terms of who writes to presidents, but when a co-partisan comes into the Whitehouse, members appear to write more to executive-branch agencies.




Do we want to look at corporate PAC contributions? Do we want to look at contributions from the industry areas that the agency covers? Not sure how easy it would be to do that crosswalk.





## What it looks like


```{r}
#| label: fig-words
#| fig-cap: "Most frequent words by year"

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", "wordcloud", type, "overall_by_year.png"))
```

Given that its mission is to support small businesses in the United States, the Small Business Administration is a locus of communications from members of Congress on behalf of businesses. Many of these involved requests for disaster assistance, as well as assistance with accessing capital and obtaining loans. Legislators also wrote on behalf of constituents seeking assistance with the 8(a) Business Development Program, which provides support to business owners who are presumed to be socially disadvantaged, including Black Americans, Hispanic Americans, Native Americans, and Asian Americans. Requests on behalf of small businesses appeared in many other agencies as well. Many legislators wrote on behalf of businesses that needed assistance with work visas and other documentation for foreign workers, and these communications appeared in a number of different agencies, including USCIS and several sub-agencies in the Department of Labor like the Employment and Training Administration (ETA) and the Office of the Solicitor. Other requests on behalf of businesses that legislators sent to DOL agencies involved disputes over fines that businesses received and help with Trade Adjustment Assistance (TAA) benefits for displaced workers. 

As with individual constituency service, there are examples of business-focused constituency service that are less obvious or focus less on bureaucratic problems and government benefits. For example, Senator Richard Lugar wrote to the FDA on behalf of a constituent who owned a produce company and was concerned about how the classification of watermelons in a high-risk food safety category would affect the productivity of his business, given the extra (and in his view, unnecessary) precautions that the company would have to take. Although the business owner was technically expressing an opinion about FDA policy in this communication with Lugar, the issue at hand had a clear and tangible impact upon his business operations, and the constituent presumably wanted the FDA to reconsider the policy to mitigate these effects. As another example, Representative Eddie Bernice Johnson wrote to the IRS on behalf of a constituent who had submitted an application to the IRS with an error that needed to be corrected (the constituent mistakenly wrote his PIN number instead of his PTIN number on the form). The constituent had contacted Johnson's office because the paperwork had still not been processed after he re-submitted a version with the correction, and he was concerned about "losing money in lost potential business." Both of these contacts show that legislators' advocacy on behalf of businesses is not necessarily about fixing bureaucratic problems or obtaining special treatment for businesses at the expense of the public good.


## Explaining Differences in Representing Businesses  

Variation in constituency service is mainly driven by ... 

### Demand

```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-demand
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of demand on requests to federal agencies"
#| out-width: 100%

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_demand.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_demand,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
                          "This table shows estimates the relationship between district demographics that may affect demand and levels of interbranch representation.")) 
```




```{r}
#| label: fig-pop-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation per Senator per year compared to counterfactual of equal state population"
#| out-width: 50%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-population-Senate-effect-1.png"))

#TOD college grads and government workers 

```


### Capacity

Columns 1, 3, and 5 of @tbl-models-demand) show differences across legislators with different capacity advantages. 

```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-capacity
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of capacity on total requests to federal agencies"
#| out-width: 100%

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_capacity.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_capacity,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of legislator capacity (committee chair, subcommittee chair, and ranking member positions and years in office) and institutional positions that may affect strategic behavior (oversight committee membership, majority party status, co-partisan president) on levels of interbranch representation.")) 
```


```{r}
#| label: fig-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation per legislator per year compared to counterfactuals"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(
here::here("figs", type,
           paste0("m-effect-",
                  c(1:2,5,8), 
                  ".png"
                  ) 
           ))
```




##### Staff quality 

```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-district
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of capacity on total requests to federal agencies per district"

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_district.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_district,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by district",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of electing a new member of the same or different party on levels of interbranch representation.")) 
```


### Strategic Responses to the Environment


#### Interbranch representation as electoral strategy


```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-elect
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of electoral security on total requests to federal agencies"
#| out-width: 60%

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_elect.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_elect,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of electoral competition on levels of interbranch representation. "))  

```


```{r}
#| label: fig-elect-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation compared to counterfactual of no electoral competition"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-elect-effect-1.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-elect-did-effect-2.png"))

```



#### Strategic responses to partisan institutions


 


```{r}
#| label: fig-ideo-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation compared to counterfactual of no difference in legislator ideology (a) and perceived agency ideology (b)"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-ideo-effect-1.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-ideo-effect-2.png"))
```




```{r}
#| label: fig-partisan-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation compared to counterfactuals of no partisan effects"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-effect-6.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-did-effect-6.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-effect-7.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-did-effect-7.png"))
```




#### Staff allocation reflects priories


```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-staff
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of staff budgeting on requests to federal agencies"
#| out-width: 100%

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_staff_spending.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_staff_spending,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of legislator staff spending. ")) 
```



```{r}
#| label: fig-effect-staff
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation per representative per year compared to counterfactual staffing levels"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(
here::here("figs", type,
           paste0("m-staff_spending-effect-",
                  c(1:2), 
                  ".png"
                  ) 
           ))
```



#### Interbranch representation and campaign fundraising strategy


```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-pac
#| tbl-cap: "Legislator requests to federal agencies and Corporate Political Action Committee Contributions in the Prior Congress" 

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_pac.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_pac,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by district",
                          "This table shows estimates of the relationship between corporate Political Action Committee donations and levels of interbranch representation.")) 
```
